points: A quadratic equation in variable x is of the form ax² + bx + c = 0, where a, b, c are real numbers and a ≠ 0.
The term 'quadratic' refers to the degree 2 (the highest power of x). If a = 0 after simplification, the equation is linear, not quadratic.
Many real-life situations (dimensions, motion, profit, age) are modeled as quadratic equations.
The factorisation method expresses the quadratic as a product of two linear factors, then sets each factor to zero and solves.
The middle term splitting technique (finding two numbers whose product is ac and sum is b) helps factorise the quadratic expression.
If factorisation is not easily possible, the quadratic formula is used instead.
The quadratic formula is x = [-b ± √(b² - 4ac)] / (2a), which gives the roots of any quadratic equation.
The discriminant Δ = b² - 4ac determines the nature of roots: if Δ > 0, two distinct real roots; if Δ = 0, two equal real roots; if Δ < 0, no real roots.
The discriminant also has geometric significance: it determines whether the parabola y = ax² + bx + c intersects the x-axis at two points, one point, or not at all.
Word problems involving two unknowns are translated into quadratic equations by defining variables and setting up equations based on given conditions.
Both roots obtained from solving must be verified for contextual validity; negative lengths, negative ages, or impossible values must be rejected.
Common applications include finding dimensions of rectangles, determining ages, solving geometry problems involving circles and triangles, and optimizing profit and cost.
